title: Removing James E. Boasberg, Chief Judge of the United States District Court for the District of Columbia, for failure to remain in good behavior pursuant to section 1 of article III of the Constitution.
plain_summary: This bill wants to remove Chief Judge James E. Boasberg from his job as a federal judge in Washington D.C. The House of Representatives says the judge did something wrong by stopping President Trump from taking action against a Venezuelan gang called Tren de Aragua. They claim the judge went beyond what he was allowed to do and interfered with the President's decisions about dealing with other countries. Federal judges are supposed to behave well to keep their jobs, and the House believes this judge failed to do that.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution aims to remove James E. Boasberg from his position as Chief Judge of the United States District Court for the District of Columbia. The reason given is that he failed to remain in good behavior as required by section one of article three of the Constitution.

sections.title: Resolution Statement
sections.text: The House of Representatives has decided that James E. Boasberg should be removed from his position as Chief Judge of the United States District Court for the District of Columbia. They say he failed to meet the standard of good behavior that judges must maintain under section one of article three of the Constitution. The House will present this article of removal to the United States Senate.

sections.title: Article of Removal Introduction
sections.text: This article of removal is presented by the House of Representatives of the United States of America in the name of itself and all the people of the United States. It is against James E. Boasberg, Chief Judge of the United States District Court for the District of Columbia. The House supports removing him because he failed to remain in good behavior.

sections.title: Article I: Abuse of Power
sections.text: Chief Judge Boasberg is accused of breaking his oath of office. The House claims he knowingly and willfully used his position as a judge to interfere with the President's constitutional powers and enforcement of the law for political gain. The Constitution gives Congress the power to create, eliminate, and regulate all federal courts below the Supreme Court. Section one of article three of the Constitution says that judges of both the Supreme Court and lower courts shall hold their offices during good behavior. This gives Congress the authority to remove a judge who fails to remain in good behavior while in office. The Constitution gives the President broad and extensive powers over foreign policy and ensuring national security against foreign threats. The Supreme Court has ruled that the power to conduct foreign affairs is a political power that is largely beyond the scope of judicial inquiry or decision. Therefore, except for questions about whether a provision or action is constitutional, the Constitution places foreign policy decisions by the President and Congress beyond the scope of review by the courts, along with actions taken based on those decisions. In seventeen ninety eight, Congress passed the Alien Enemies Act. This law allows the President to make a public announcement identifying members of a foreign nation or government who can be arrested and removed as alien enemies of the United States. The Act gives the President sole authority to determine how these enemy aliens should be restrained and removed, as well as to establish any regulations necessary to ensure public safety. On March fifteenth, twenty twenty five, President Donald J. Trump issued a proclamation declaring that all Venezuelan citizens who are members of the organization known as Tren de Aragua can be arrested and removed under the Alien Enemies Act. This is because the organization poses a threat to the United States and has clear connections with the government of Venezuela. Furthermore, because of the severity of Tren de Aragua's crimes against the United States, the President declared that members of Tren de Aragua are not eligible for the benefits of section twenty two of the Act. Therefore, they can be immediately arrested and removed. Based on this declaration, the President directed the Attorney General and the Secretary of Homeland Security to immediately take steps necessary to begin arresting and removing members of Tren de Aragua who are present within the United States. Despite the broad authority given by the Constitution to the President when conducting matters of foreign affairs and national security, and the clear authority given to the President under the Alien Enemies Act, James E. Boasberg knowingly interfered with the President's execution of foreign policy. He did this by ordering the return of members of a designated foreign terrorist organization to the United States. To justify his action, Boasberg cited questions concerning the interpretation of certain words and phrases that are closely tied to the President's foreign policy powers. These are therefore beyond the scope of review by the courts. In doing so, James E. Boasberg knowingly went beyond the limits of power of his office and unjustly interfered in the execution of foreign policy and national security for partisan purposes. His goal was to halt the implementation of the President's foreign policy and for political gain. Furthermore, James E. Boasberg has failed to disclose payments received from non federal sources and abused his discretion during his term on the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Court (a special court that handles certain national security cases). Accordingly, under the good behavior clause of section one of article three, these actions of James E. Boasberg constitute misbehavior and make him liable for removal from office. Therefore, James E. Boasberg, by his conduct, has failed to remain in good behavior and should be removed from office and no longer be considered a judge of the United States.
